past_medical_history: Atrial fibrillation, diagnosed 5 years ago, previously on warfarin but self-discontinued 4 months ago due to concerns about bruising
Hypertension, diagnosed 18 years ago, poorly controlled
Type 2 diabetes mellitus, diagnosed 11 years ago
Congestive heart failure with preserved ejection fraction (HFpEF), diagnosed 2 years ago
Obstructive sleep apnea, uses CPAP intermittently
Chronic kidney disease stage 3a (baseline creatinine 1.3 mg/dL)

medications.indication: Heart failure and lower extremity edema
Cardiovascular risk reduction (substituted by patient after stopping warfarin)

allergies: Iodinated contrast dye (mild nausea, premedication protocol in place)
family_history: Mother had a stroke at age 71 and died from complications at age 73
Father had type 2 diabetes and coronary artery disease, died at age 69 from myocardial infarction
Sister with hypertension and atrial fibrillation

social_history.alcohol: Rare social drinker, approximately 1-2 glasses of wine per month
social_history.living_situation: Lives alone in a two-story townhouse, daughter lives 15 minutes away
social_history.exercise: Minimal, walks to mailbox daily but limited by dyspnea on exertion and bilateral knee pain
social_history.diet: Reports difficulty following diabetic diet, admits to high-sodium and processed food intake

neurological_exam.mental_status: Alert, appears frightened. Oriented to person and place but not to date. Expressive aphasia: speaks in short, effortful phrases with word-finding pauses and phonemic paraphasias. Comprehension relatively preserved for simple commands. Unable to repeat phrases. Cannot name low-frequency objects (e.g., calls a watch strap a 'band thing'). Unable to write a complete sentence. Reading impaired for complex sentences.
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: Pupils 3 mm bilaterally, equal and reactive. Left gaze preference with full range on oculocephalic maneuver. Right upper motor neuron facial weakness with flattening of the right nasolabial fold, forehead spared. Hearing grossly intact bilaterally. Palate elevates symmetrically. Tongue protrudes to the right.
neurological_exam.motor: Right upper extremity: severe weakness, 1/5 deltoid, 1/5 biceps, 0/5 wrist extension, 0/5 grip. Right lower extremity: 2/5 hip flexion, 2/5 knee extension, 1/5 ankle dorsiflexion. Left upper and lower extremities: 5/5 strength throughout. Right arm drifts and falls to bed immediately on pronator drift testing.
neurological_exam.sensory: Diminished light touch and pinprick sensation over the right face, arm, and leg. Right-sided extinction on double simultaneous stimulation in both visual and tactile modalities. Proprioception impaired in right fingers.
neurological_exam.reflexes: Left upper extremity 2+, left lower extremity 2+. Right upper extremity 3+, right lower extremity 3+ with sustained clonus at the right ankle (4 beats). Right plantar response extensor (positive Babinski). Left plantar response flexor (normal).
neurological_exam.coordination: Unable to assess on the right due to severe weakness. Left finger-to-nose and heel-to-shin intact without dysmetria.
neurological_exam.gait: Unable to stand or ambulate due to right hemiparesis.
neurological_exam.additional: NIHSS score: 16. Right visual field cut to confrontation (right homonymous hemianopia). Mild right-sided neglect on visual extinction testing. No meningismus. No carotid bruits auscultated.

patient.chief_complaint: Sudden onset difficulty speaking and right-sided weakness
patient.history_present_illness: Mrs. Doreen Caldwell is a 68-year-old left-handed African American woman with a history of atrial fibrillation (self-discontinued warfarin 4 months ago), poorly controlled hypertension, type 2 diabetes on insulin and oral agents, HFpEF, and CKD stage 3a, who was brought by ambulance to the emergency department after her daughter called 911 when she arrived for a scheduled visit and found her mother on the living room floor. The daughter reports speaking to her mother by phone at approximately 09:00 this morning, at which time she sounded entirely normal. The daughter arrived at the home at 10:45 and found the front door unlocked. She discovered her mother sitting on the floor next to the couch, leaning against it, with her right arm limp at her side. Mrs. Caldwell was attempting to speak but her words were garbled and she appeared confused and frightened. There was no evidence of head trauma, urinary incontinence, or tongue biting. No vomiting was reported. EMS arrived at 10:58 and documented right-sided hemiparesis with facial droop and expressive aphasia. A prehospital stroke alert was activated. Prehospital fingerstick glucose was 202 mg/dL. The cardiac monitor showed an irregularly irregular rhythm without distinct P waves, consistent with atrial fibrillation. She was transported to our comprehensive stroke center with arrival at 11:22. On ED assessment, Mrs. Caldwell was alert but distressed, speaking in short effortful phrases. She was able to state her name but could not reliably provide her birthdate or describe what happened. Her daughter confirmed that the last known well time was 09:00, making the symptom duration approximately 2 hours and 22 minutes at time of ED arrival, well within the thrombolytic treatment window. Her daughter also confirmed that Mrs. Caldwell stopped taking warfarin approximately four months ago because she was developing large bruises on her arms and was worried about falling and bleeding.

findings.location: Left middle cerebral artery territory involving the left frontal operculum (Broca area), left precentral gyrus, left insular cortex, and left parietal operculum
findings.size: Approximately 4.6 x 3.2 cm in maximal axial dimensions

signal_characteristics.DWI: Markedly hyperintense signal indicating restricted diffusion
signal_characteristics.ADC: Corresponding hypointensity confirming true restricted diffusion
signal_characteristics.FLAIR: Mild hyperintensity in the affected territory, consistent with subacute onset (>2 hours)
signal_characteristics.T1: Subtle hypointensity in the left frontal and parietal opercular regions
signal_characteristics.T2: Hyperintense signal corresponding to the DWI abnormality

findings.mass_effect: Mild sulcal effacement in the left frontoparietal region. No midline shift. No uncal herniation.
findings.borders: Well-demarcated margins conforming to the left MCA superior division territory

findings.location: Left MCA M1 segment with extension into the superior division (M2)

signal_characteristics.MRA: Abrupt cutoff of the left MCA at the distal M1 segment with absent flow signal in the superior division
signal_characteristics.TOF: Loss of flow-related enhancement in the left MCA superior division

additional_observations: Moderate periventricular and subcortical white matter T2/FLAIR hyperintensities consistent with chronic small vessel ischemic disease (Fazekas grade 2-3)
Right MCA, bilateral ACA, and bilateral PCA are patent on MRA
Mild to moderate generalized cerebral volume loss, slightly beyond expected for age
Small chronic lacunar infarct in the right caudate head
mri.impression: 1. Acute left MCA territory infarction with restricted diffusion involving the left frontal operculum (Broca area), precentral gyrus, insular cortex, and parietal operculum, consistent with left MCA superior division territory. 2. Left MCA M1/superior M2 division occlusion on MRA. 3. No hemorrhagic transformation. 4. Moderate chronic small vessel ischemic disease and remote right caudate lacunar infarct. Findings are consistent with acute large vessel occlusion ischemic stroke. Urgent consideration for IV thrombolysis and mechanical thrombectomy is recommended.

differential_by_imaging.diagnosis: Acute ischemic infarction, left MCA superior division territory
differential_by_imaging.key_features: DWI restriction with ADC confirmation in a vascular territory distribution with corresponding MCA occlusion on MRA
No intraparenchymal hemorrhage, no subarachnoid hemorrhage on any sequence

recommended_actions: Urgent neurology and neurointerventional consultation for IV tPA and mechanical thrombectomy evaluation
CT angiography of head and neck for detailed vascular characterization
Follow-up imaging in 24 hours to evaluate for hemorrhagic transformation

findings: Atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response
Left atrial enlargement by P-terminal force criteria (though fibrillatory baseline)
Left ventricular hypertrophy by Sokolow-Lyon criteria
Nonspecific ST-T wave changes in leads V4-V6
No acute ST elevation or depression
ecg.interpretation: Atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response at 106 bpm. Left ventricular hypertrophy by voltage criteria. Nonspecific lateral ST-T wave changes, likely related to LVH. No acute ischemic ECG changes.
ecg.clinical_correlation: Atrial fibrillation without anticoagulation is the most likely cardioembolic source for this patient's acute ischemic stroke. The LVH is consistent with her longstanding poorly controlled hypertension. Rate control is warranted.

BMP.clinical_significance: Elevated, consistent with known CKD stage 3a and possible dehydration due to diuretic use
Mildly above baseline (1.3), consistent with CKD 3a; consider hydration status with furosemide use
Elevated, consistent with poorly controlled diabetes and acute stress hyperglycemia

Coagulation.clinical_significance: Normal INR confirms patient is not therapeutically anticoagulated, consistent with reported discontinuation of warfarin

Lipid Panel.clinical_significance: Elevated despite statin therapy, suboptimal control
Above target for high-risk patient with multiple vascular risk factors
Low for female sex, additional cardiovascular risk factor
Elevated, associated with metabolic syndrome and insulin resistance

Cardiac and Other.clinical_significance: Mildly elevated, may reflect demand ischemia in the setting of AF with RVR and acute stroke
Poorly controlled diabetes, significant modifiable stroke risk factor
Elevated, consistent with known HFpEF and volume overload status

labs.interpretation: Laboratory results demonstrate mildly elevated BUN and creatinine consistent with known CKD 3a (near baseline), hyperglycemia reflecting poorly controlled diabetes (HbA1c 8.9%), and suboptimal lipid control despite statin therapy. Coagulation studies are normal, confirming the patient is not anticoagulated. Mildly elevated troponin likely represents demand ischemia. Elevated BNP is consistent with known heart failure. CBC is unremarkable.
abnormal_values_summary: BUN 28 mg/dL (elevated, CKD and diuretic use)

findings.location: Left MCA at the distal M1 segment extending into the superior M2 division

signal_characteristics.CTA: Occlusion of the left MCA at the distal M1 segment with thrombus extending into the superior M2 division. Inferior M2 division appears patent with diminished opacification.

findings.location: Bilateral carotid bifurcations and left vertebral artery origin

signal_characteristics.CTA: Mixed calcified and soft plaque at bilateral carotid bifurcations. Right ICA with approximately 30% stenosis. Left ICA with approximately 45% stenosis. Left vertebral artery origin with mild stenosis (<30%).

additional_observations: Right MCA is patent without stenosis
Bilateral vertebral arteries patent with right vertebral artery dominant
Basilar artery patent without significant stenosis
No evidence of cervical vessel dissection
Aortic arch is type III (bovine variant with common origin of brachiocephalic and left common carotid arteries)
output.impression: 1. Left MCA distal M1/superior M2 division occlusion consistent with large vessel occlusion amenable to mechanical thrombectomy. 2. Mild to moderate bilateral carotid bifurcation atherosclerotic disease without hemodynamically significant stenosis. 3. No cervical vessel dissection. 4. Type III aortic arch variant noted for procedural planning.
recommended_actions: IV alteplase as bridge to thrombectomy if within time window and no contraindications
Note type III aortic arch for neurointerventional access planning

Transthoracic Echocardiogram.clinical_significance: Preserved ejection fraction, consistent with known HFpEF
Severely dilated left atrium, consistent with longstanding atrial fibrillation and diastolic dysfunction
Consistent with chronic poorly controlled hypertension
Likely functional due to annular dilation from left atrial enlargement
Estimated RVSP 39 mmHg, mild pulmonary hypertension

output.interpretation: Transthoracic echocardiogram reveals preserved left ventricular systolic function (LVEF 58%) with concentric LVH and grade II diastolic dysfunction, consistent with heart failure with preserved ejection fraction. Severely dilated left atrium (LAVI 42 mL/m2) is consistent with longstanding atrial fibrillation and serves as a substrate for thrombus formation. Moderate functional mitral regurgitation. Mild pulmonary hypertension. No definite intracardiac thrombus visualized on TTE, though TEE is recommended for definitive left atrial appendage evaluation. Overall findings strongly support cardioembolic mechanism.

findings: Persistent atrial fibrillation throughout the 48-hour recording period
No periods of sinus rhythm identified
Maximum heart rate 134 bpm during physical therapy session
Minimum heart rate 58 bpm during sleep
Two pauses noted, longest 2.1 seconds, both during sleep
Occasional premature ventricular complexes (78 in 48 hours, <0.1% burden)
No sustained or non-sustained ventricular tachycardia
output.interpretation: Forty-eight hour Holter monitoring confirms persistent atrial fibrillation with overall adequate rate control (average 92 bpm). No sinus rhythm captured. No significant pauses or ventricular arrhythmias.
output.clinical_correlation: Persistent atrial fibrillation confirmed, underscoring the need for systemic anticoagulation for secondary stroke prevention. Rate control appears adequate on current regimen, though daytime rates are on the higher side.

Hypercoagulability Panel.clinical_significance: Mildly elevated homocysteine, may be related to CKD; consider folate and B12 supplementation

output.interpretation: Hypercoagulability panel is essentially normal with the exception of mildly elevated homocysteine, which is likely related to the patient's chronic kidney disease. No hereditary or acquired thrombophilia identified. The stroke etiology remains most likely cardioembolic given the known persistent atrial fibrillation without anticoagulation.

findings.location: Left internal carotid artery at the bifurcation

signal_characteristics.Plaque_characteristics: Heterogeneous plaque with mixed echogenicity, predominantly calcified

findings.location: Right internal carotid artery at the bifurcation

additional_observations: Bilateral common carotid arteries demonstrate mildly elevated peak systolic velocities consistent with hypertensive vasculopathy
Bilateral vertebral arteries are patent with antegrade flow
Increased intima-media thickness bilaterally (0.9 mm), consistent with generalized atherosclerosis
output.impression: 1. Mild (<50%) stenosis of the left internal carotid artery. 2. Minimal (<30%) stenosis of the right internal carotid artery. 3. Patent bilateral vertebral arteries. 4. Generalized atherosclerotic changes. The carotid disease is not hemodynamically significant and is unlikely to be the primary stroke mechanism. Cardioembolic etiology (atrial fibrillation) remains the leading cause.
recommended_actions: Optimize atherosclerotic risk factor management (statin intensification, blood pressure control, diabetes management)
Anticoagulation for cardioembolic stroke prevention takes precedence
Consider repeat carotid imaging in 12 months to monitor progression

interactions: Aspirin (current medication): Increased bleeding risk with concurrent anticoagulant and antiplatelet therapy. Discontinue aspirin when initiating apixaban for AF-related stroke prevention unless dual indication exists.
Glipizide: No significant pharmacokinetic interaction, but monitor for hypoglycemia as concurrent illness and dietary changes during hospitalization may affect glucose control.
contraindications: CrCl should be assessed; dose reduction to 2.5 mg twice daily if two of three criteria met: age >=80, weight <=60 kg, creatinine >=1.5 mg/dL. Patient meets one criterion (creatinine 1.4 mg/dL, borderline). Standard dose of 5 mg BID is appropriate at this time.
warnings: Monitor renal function periodically given CKD stage 3a; dose adjustment may be needed if renal function declines
Avoid concurrent use of aspirin unless specific indication (e.g., recent coronary stent)
Hold apixaban 48 hours before any planned invasive procedures
alternatives: Rivaroxaban 20 mg once daily (renal dose: 15 mg once daily if CrCl 15-50)
Edoxaban 60 mg once daily (dose reduce to 30 mg if CrCl 15-50 or weight <=60 kg)
Warfarin (requires INR monitoring, less convenient but well-established in CKD)

output.query: acute ischemic stroke atrial fibrillation anticoagulation timing guidelines

results.title: 2019 AHA/ASA Guideline for the Early Management of Patients With Acute Ischemic Stroke
results.authors: Powers WJ, Rabinstein AA, et al.
results.key_finding: For patients with AF-related stroke, initiation of oral anticoagulation is recommended, but optimal timing depends on stroke severity and risk of hemorrhagic transformation. Most experts recommend starting anticoagulation 4-14 days after stroke onset.

results.title: Timing of Oral Anticoagulant Therapy in Acute Ischemic Stroke With Atrial Fibrillation (ELAN Trial)
results.key_finding: Early initiation of DOACs (within 48 hours for minor strokes, within 6-7 days for moderate strokes) was noninferior to later initiation with respect to a composite of recurrent ischemic stroke, systemic embolism, major extracranial bleeding, symptomatic intracranial hemorrhage, or vascular death at 30 days.

results.title: European Stroke Organisation guidelines on antithrombotic treatment after stroke
results.key_finding: For patients with AF and moderate ischemic stroke (NIHSS 8-15), anticoagulation may be started at day 6-7. For severe stroke (NIHSS >=16), consider starting at day 12-14 after repeat imaging to exclude hemorrhagic transformation.

output.summary: Current evidence supports initiating oral anticoagulation (preferably a DOAC) in AF-related ischemic stroke patients. Timing depends on stroke severity: minor strokes (NIHSS <8) may start within 48 hours, moderate strokes within 6-7 days, and severe strokes at 12-14 days after exclusion of hemorrhagic transformation on repeat imaging. The ELAN trial supports earlier initiation as safe. For this patient with NIHSS 16 (severe), starting anticoagulation around day 7-14 with repeat imaging at day 5-7 to exclude hemorrhagic transformation would be appropriate.

ground_truth.primary_diagnosis: Acute ischemic stroke, left middle cerebral artery superior division territory, cardioembolic etiology due to atrial fibrillation

differential.key_distinguishing: CT/MRI shows no hemorrhage; DWI restriction in vascular territory with MRA occlusion confirms ischemic etiology
No witnessed seizure, no history of epilepsy, persistent deficits without improvement, and MRI confirms acute infarction
Age 68 with no migraine history, persistent deficits with imaging confirmation of infarction
Prehospital glucose 202 mg/dL and ED glucose 218 mg/dL; hypoglycemia is excluded
MRI shows vascular territory DWI restriction without enhancing mass; pattern is classic for acute ischemic stroke

optimal_actions.action: Activate stroke code and establish last known well time; perform rapid NIHSS assessment and obtain fingerstick glucose
Obtain emergent non-contrast CT head to rule out intracranial hemorrhage
optimal_actions.expected_finding: No hemorrhage; possible subtle early ischemic changes in left MCA territory

optimal_actions.action: Obtain stat labs including CBC, BMP, coagulation studies, troponin, and fingerstick glucose
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Normal coagulation (INR 1.1 confirms patient is not anticoagulated), hyperglycemia, mildly elevated creatinine near baseline
Atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response confirming cardioembolic source

optimal_actions.action: Administer IV alteplase 0.9 mg/kg (max 90 mg) with 10% as bolus and remainder over 60 minutes; patient is within the 4.5-hour window with no contraindications
Obtain CTA head and neck to evaluate for large vessel occlusion
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Left MCA distal M1/superior M2 occlusion amenable to thrombectomy

optimal_actions.action: Consult neurointerventional surgery for mechanical thrombectomy given confirmed large vessel occlusion
Obtain brain MRI with DWI to define infarct extent
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Acute left MCA superior division territory infarction with restricted diffusion

optimal_actions.action: Lower blood pressure to below 185/110 prior to thrombolysis, then maintain below 180/105 for 24 hours post-tPA
Admit to neurological ICU or dedicated stroke unit for continuous monitoring
Initiate DOAC (apixaban 5 mg BID) for secondary stroke prevention once hemorrhagic transformation excluded on repeat imaging at day 7-14, given NIHSS 16 (severe stroke)
Order transthoracic echocardiogram for cardiac structural evaluation
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Preserved EF with dilated left atrium and diastolic dysfunction consistent with HFpEF and AF substrate

optimal_actions.action: Optimize management of vascular risk factors: intensify statin, improve diabetes control, achieve blood pressure targets

critical_actions: Establish last known well time to determine thrombolysis eligibility
Obtain emergent non-contrast CT head to exclude hemorrhage before thrombolysis
Administer IV alteplase within the 4.5-hour treatment window
Identify large vessel occlusion via CTA and consult for mechanical thrombectomy
Obtain ECG to identify atrial fibrillation as the embolic source
Manage blood pressure to safe thrombolysis range (below 185/110 pre-tPA)
Initiate anticoagulation (DOAC) for secondary prevention once acute phase permits
Monitor in ICU/stroke unit with serial neurological assessments
contraindicated_actions: Administering anticoagulation (heparin, DOAC) acutely before excluding hemorrhagic transformation
Aggressively lowering blood pressure below 140/90 in the hyperacute phase (risk of extending ischemic penumbra)
Administering antiplatelet agents (aspirin, clopidogrel) concurrently with IV alteplase within 24 hours
Delaying IV alteplase to obtain MRI when the patient is within the thrombolytic window
Administering full-dose IV contrast without premedication given the patient's documented contrast allergy
Performing lumbar puncture in the setting of acute large territory stroke
Continuing aspirin once anticoagulation with DOAC is initiated (unless specific dual indication exists)
key_reasoning_points: Sudden onset expressive aphasia with right hemiparesis in a left-handed patient with left-hemisphere dominance for language (confirmed by aphasia pattern) localizes to the left MCA superior division territory
Known atrial fibrillation with self-discontinued anticoagulation is the clear cardioembolic mechanism, confirmed by AF on ECG and normal INR indicating subtherapeutic anticoagulation
Last known well time of 09:00 with ED arrival at 11:22 places this patient within the 4.5-hour IV alteplase treatment window
MRI demonstrates DWI hyperintensity with ADC confirmation in left MCA superior division territory with corresponding M1/M2 occlusion on MRA, classic for acute LVO ischemic stroke
NIHSS of 16 indicates severe stroke and mandates consideration of mechanical thrombectomy in addition to IV thrombolysis
Multiple comorbidities (CKD, HFpEF, poorly controlled DM) affect treatment planning: apixaban dosing must account for renal function, contrast allergy requires premedication for CTA
Blood pressure of 192/104 must be lowered to below 185/110 before administering IV alteplase
Left-handedness raises the question of right-hemisphere language dominance, but the presence of clear expressive aphasia confirms left-hemisphere dominance in this patient

metadata.difficulty_description: Straightforward - classic presentation with sudden onset right hemiparesis and expressive aphasia, known AF self-discontinued from anticoagulation, clear LVO on imaging
clinical_pearls: African Americans have nearly twice the rate of first stroke compared to white Americans, partly attributable to higher prevalence of hypertension, diabetes, and obesity
Self-discontinuation of anticoagulation is a common real-world problem; approximately 30% of AF patients discontinue OAC within the first year
Left-handedness does not reliably predict right-hemisphere language dominance; over 70% of left-handed individuals are still left-hemisphere dominant for language
CKD stage 3a requires monitoring but does not contraindicate standard-dose apixaban unless additional dose-reduction criteria are met (age >=80, weight <=60 kg, Cr >=1.5)
